banner: Week 5 · Angle Type Hunt & Reflex Build
essential_question: Can you find all five angle types in the real world — including the one that has to wrap past a straight line?
objective: Measure real-world examples of acute, right, obtuse, and straight angles, and build and measure a reflex angle using a hinge or clock face.

agenda.title: Brief — From Naming Types to Finding Them

content.heading: From the five families to five real objects
bullets: Yesterday: sorted degree measures into acute, right, obtuse, straight, and reflex.
Today: hunt one real example of each of the first four, then build the trickiest one — a reflex angle — yourself.
The test: can you measure to the nearest degree, and can you show a real angle bigger than a straight line?

content.heading: Gather + plan
content.note: Open the project card. Gather your protractor, ruler, pencil, paper, and either a real analog clock (or a printed clock face with brad-fastened hands) or two straight strips joined by a paper fastener to make an adjustable hinge. Decide which four real angles you'll hunt before you start measuring.

agenda.title: Build — Hunt the Four, Build the Fifth

content.heading: Measure the world, then build past straight
bullets: Type Hunt — find and measure one real example each of acute, right, obtuse, and straight.
Reflex Build — using your hinge or clock hands, rotate past 180° and measure the resulting reflex angle.
For every angle, mark or note the vertex and both rays, and write m∠ = its value beside it.
Re-check every measurement with the protractor before you call it done.

content.preview: Session 3: defend why the boundaries never blur, review the week's Math-U-See practice, then the checkpoint.

success_criteria.text: I can find and measure real-world examples of acute, right, obtuse, and straight angles
I can build and measure a reflex angle using a hinge or a clock face
I can explain the relationship between an angle and its reflex counterpart at the same vertex

compass.note: Curriculum/Geometry/The Salas Compass/2026-W05 Types of Angles.md

questions.prompt: List the four real-world angles you hunted (acute, right, obtuse, straight) — name each object and its measured degree, to the nearest whole degree.
questions.answer: Any four honest, plausible real objects with degree measures matching their claimed type, e.g. scissors open a crack ≈ 15° (acute), a book cover square to its spine = 90° (right), a laptop screen tipped back ≈ 110° (obtuse), a book laid flat open = 180° (straight).
questions.explain: Rubric: four named real objects (1 pt) + four degree measures that are honestly consistent with the claimed type (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Using your hinge or clock hands, rotate past 180° and measure the resulting reflex angle to the nearest whole degree. Name what you used and record the measurement.
questions.answer: Any reflex measurement strictly between 180° and 360°, e.g. clock hands at 10:00 measured the long way around ≈ 300°.
questions.explain: Rubric: names the object/tool used (1 pt) + a measured value strictly between 180° and 360° (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Your hinge or clock hands actually make two angles at once — the one you'd normally call 'the angle,' and the reflex angle on the other side. Add your two measurements together. What do they equal, and why does that make sense?
questions.answer: They add to 360°, because together the two angles sweep completely around the vertex back to the starting ray — one full turn.
questions.explain: Full credit: states the sum is 360° and connects it to 'one full turn around the vertex.'

questions.prompt: Reflect: which was harder — finding a real straight angle, or building a real reflex angle? What did building the reflex angle teach you that the definition alone didn't?
